Assistant: Sure, when Congress writes a law, it’s called a bill.  And, for a bill to become the law, it needs to pass both chambers of Congress.  For example, bills about the military are passed in the House of Representatives, and bills about taxes are passed in the Senate.  Once the bill has passed, it goes to the president.  If the president agrees with the bill, it becomes a law.  If the president doesn’t agree with the bill, he or she can choose to either sign the bill, or not sign the bill.  If the president signs the bill, then the bill becomes law.  If the president does not sign the bill, then the bill does not become law, but the bill can be passed again later.  Alternatively, if the president vetoes the bill, then it goes back to Congress.  If Congress overrides the president’s veto, then the bill becomes law.  If Congress does not override the president’s veto, then the bill does not become law.

Ingredients:

12 eggs, separated

Completos
Chilean dish

When cleaning your erasers, I recommend first soaking them in isopropyl alcohol or a bleach-water mixture, and drying them.  Next, it’s best to use a small brush to remove eraser dust. Now that your erasers are cleaned, you can put them away in a dry, cool location.

You should never use ammonia, bleach or dishwashing detergent to clean your erasers, as these may chemically interact with the dry erase material and cause the surface to deteriorate.  Isopropyl alcohol is probably the cleanest choice, and the easiest to obtain in most areas.

You'll want to make sure the batter is well-mixed, so the spices and butter are evenly distributed throughout it.

The key to making homemade gingerbread is making sure it is cooked evenly. You want to heat up your oven to 375°F, grease your baking pan, and pour the gingerbread batter into it. Make sure the pan is also well-greased, to ensure that the gingerbread cooks evenly. Then pop it into the oven, and cook it until a toothpick inserted in the center comes out clean, and the sides are starting to brown, about 15-20 minutes.

While the gingerbread is cooking, you'll want to make some sortdedged gingerbread cookies to serve alongside it. You'll want to preheat your oven to 350°F, and line a baking sheet with parchment paper or a silicone baking mat. Mix together 2 cups of all-purpose flour, 2 teaspoons of ground ginger, 1 teaspoon of ground cinnamon, 1/2 teaspoon of ground nutmeg, and a pinch of ground cloves, and 1/2 teaspoon of salt.

In a separate bowl, mix together 1/2 cup of softened butter, and 1/2 cup brown sugar. In a medium bowl or a large bowl, whisk together 1/2 cup molasses, and 1/2 teaspoon vinegar. Pour the wet mixture into the flour mixture, and stir together until the dough comes together.

Break off pieces of the dough, and roll them into balls, or roll the dough out between two sheets of parchment paper to a thickness of about 1/2-inch, and use a cookie cutter or a knife to cut out gingerbread cookies. Place them on the baking sheet, spaced about 2 inches apart, and bake them for 10-12 minutes, until they're golden brown.

You'll want to mix together until it's combined, and spread it over the gingerbread in the pan.

You'll want to beat this mixture until it's smooth and fluffy, and then spoon it on top of the gingerbread house, and spread it into the edges, so it's nicely covered. You can garnish the gingerbread with a sliced gingerbread man cookie, and some gingersnaps to serve alongside it.

This should make two 8x8 inch pans of gingerbread, and one 9x13 inch pan. To serve, you'll want to place one of the pans on a plate, and serve the gingerbread with the gingerbread cookies alongside it, and the ice cream topping in a bowl on the side. The second pan should be served in the baking dish it's baked in.

The idea is that when the universe was very young, it was so hot that electrons and protons that would have otherwise combined were broken up by the heat, giving the universe a particular radiation signature.  We’re actually detecting this radiation indirectly, by using satellites to study the microwave background from a distance.  We can also study other components of the universe, like galaxies and black holes, to make deductions about the universe’s age.  As the universe gets older, we expect to see some of the smaller galaxies collapsing into black holes.  We’ve also observed galaxies colliding with each other, giving us an idea of how galaxies formed.  These are just some of the observational clues that support the big bang theory.

rejected:
Thank you for bringing this up.  There are many great ways to compost food scraps, and you should be able to look into a couple of different options in your area, but I think the best one is vermiculture, which is similar to worm farming.  The basic idea is to create an environment that will attract different types of soil-eating worms, so they can feed on your food scraps.  Different soil-eating worms are adapted to different types of food scraps, so you have to introduce several different kinds of worms, and design your environment so they can eat your food scraps without becoming cannibals.  

In an ideal world, you’ll get enough worms that they’ll “recycle” your scraps and don’t create any garbage.  You can grow plants that attract worms, and their waste will become soil.  But you should also make sure you’re giving the worms an environment that’s too hot or too wet, so they can’t reproduce too much and end up rotting your food scraps.
